era_context: The late 17th century: The Glorious Revolution in England (1688–1689) marks a turning point in English and European history. The War of the League of Augsburg (1688–1697) begins, pitting France against a coalition of European powers. The Qing Dynasty is at its height under the Kangxi Emperor. The Ottoman Empire is in slow decline. The Scientific Revolution is in full swing, with Newton's Principia published just a year earlier.

events.title: Glorious Revolution: William III and Mary II ascend to English throne
events.description: The Glorious Revolution of 1688 saw the deposition of the Catholic King James II and the ascension of his Protestant daughter Mary II and her husband William III of Orange. William invaded England in November 1688 with a Dutch army, and James II fled to France without a major battle. The revolution was bloodless in England itself, though it led to prolonged conflict in Ireland and Scotland. The revolution established the principle of parliamentary supremacy and constitutional monarchy, though the extent of these changes is debated by historians. The revolution was driven by Protestant fears of Catholic succession and aristocratic concerns about royal prerogative.

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by English nobles and observers

events.certainty_note: The Glorious Revolution is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the 17th century. The dates and major events are well-established.

events.title: War of the League of Augsburg begins: France vs. European coalition
events.description: The War of the League of Augsburg (1688–1697) began in 1688, pitting France under Louis XIV against a coalition of European powers including the Holy Roman Empire, Spain, England (after the Glorious Revolution), the Dutch Republic, and various German states. The war was fought on multiple fronts—in the Rhineland, the Low Countries, Italy, and at sea. The war was economically devastating and resulted in significant territorial changes, though the Peace of Ryswick (1697) largely restored the status quo ante. The war demonstrated the limits of French power and the effectiveness of coalition warfare.

sources.name: Military records from France, England, the Dutch Republic, and the Holy Roman Empire

events.certainty_note: The War of the League of Augsburg is extensively documented in military and diplomatic records. The dates and major campaigns are well-established.

events.title: Siege of Derry begins: Williamite War in Ireland
events.description: The Siege of Derry (or Londonderry) began in December 1688 as part of the Williamite War in Ireland. Jacobite forces under James II besieged the Protestant city of Derry, which was defended by Protestant settlers and soldiers. The siege lasted 105 days (ending in August 1690) and became a foundational event in Protestant Irish identity. The siege is commemorated annually on August 12 in Protestant tradition. The siege demonstrated the religious and political divisions that would characterize Irish history for the next two centuries.

sources.name: George Walker's 'A True Account of the Siege of Londonderry' (1689)

events.certainty_note: The siege is extensively documented in contemporary accounts and later chronicles. The dates are well-established.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor's reign: Qing Dynasty at its height
events.description: By 1688, the Kangxi Emperor had consolidated power following the suppression of the Rebellion of the Three Feudatories (1673–1681). The year 1688 marks the beginning of the Kangxi Emperor's most productive period, during which he implemented administrative reforms, expanded the civil service examination system, and began to project Qing power into Mongolia and Central Asia. The Kangxi Emperor's reign (1661–1722) is considered one of the most successful in Chinese history, marked by territorial expansion, cultural patronage, and economic growth.

sources.name: Veritable Records of the Kangxi Emperor (Kangxi Shilu)

events.certainty_note: The Kangxi Emperor's reign is well-documented in Qing imperial records.

events.title: Genroku era begins in Japan (1688–1704)
events.description: The Genroku era (1688–1704) began in 1688, marking the beginning of a period considered the height of Edo period culture. The Genroku era is characterized by the flourishing of kabuki theater, ukiyo-e woodblock prints, haiku poetry, and merchant culture. The poet Matsuo Bashō was active and influential during this period. The Tokugawa shogunate under Tsunayoshi maintained political stability, allowing for cultural and economic development in major cities like Edo, Kyoto, and Osaka.

sources.name: Genroku-era literary works and kabuki records

events.certainty_note: The Genroku era is well-documented in Japanese historical records and literary sources.

events.title: Ottoman Empire: Russo-Ottoman conflicts and territorial losses
events.description: By 1688, the Ottoman Empire was experiencing significant military setbacks against Russia and the Holy Roman Empire. The Great Turkish War (1683–1699) was underway, with Ottoman forces losing ground in Hungary and the Balkans. The Ottoman Empire's decline from its 16th-century height was becoming increasingly apparent. The year 1688 saw continued Ottoman losses and the beginning of a long period of territorial contraction that would characterize the next two centuries of Ottoman history.
key_figures: Ottoman Sultan Mehmed IV (or his successor)

sources.name: Ottoman records from the Topkapi Palace archives
Russian and Holy Roman military records

events.certainty_note: The Ottoman military situation in 1688 is documented in Ottoman, Russian, and Holy Roman records, though specific engagements may have uncertain dating.

disconfirming_evidence: The Glorious Revolution is often portrayed as a triumph of parliamentary democracy and constitutional monarchy, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the revolution was primarily a political coup by English aristocrats and Protestant interests, not a popular uprising. The revolution did not establish universal suffrage, protect religious freedom in a modern sense, or establish the full separation of powers. The extent to which the revolution established parliamentary supremacy is debated—some historians argue that the real shift toward parliamentary power occurred gradually over the 18th century, not immediately in 1688. Additionally, the revolution's 'bloodlessness' in England obscures the prolonged and bloody conflicts in Ireland and Scotland that followed.
historiographic_note: The year 1688 is exceptionally well-documented for European and English history, with rich archival sources for political, military, and intellectual history. The Glorious Revolution is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the 17th century. East Asian sources (Qing China, Japan) are also strong. However, the historical record remains heavily weighted toward literate, centralized societies with written record-keeping. The Glorious Revolution has been subject to significant reinterpretation over the past 50 years, with earlier accounts emphasizing its role in establishing democracy and recent work emphasizing its role in establishing a new form of aristocratic oligarchy and its connection to colonial expansion.

graph_edges.note: Glorious Revolution led to the Convention Parliament and the Bill of Rights in 1689
Glorious Revolution led to the Battle of the Boyne in 1690
War of the League of Augsburg ended with the Peace of Ryswick in 1697
Siege of Derry was relieved in 1690
